summary: Joel Spolsky raises alarm about Microsoft Passport, arguing it's a thinly disguised attempt to build the world's largest consumer database for profit while being marketed as a consumer benefit. He links to a longer article detailing the privacy threats. He also includes a humorous aside mocking a Concorde passenger's misunderstanding of basic probability — the gambler's fallacy that a prior crash makes a second crash less likely.
key_points: Microsoft Passport poses a major privacy threat by centralizing consumer data under Microsoft's control
Microsoft is successfully marketing a data-mining tool as a consumer benefit, and people are falling for it
The Passport system will make existing cookie-based tracking seem trivial by comparison
People commonly misunderstand probability, believing past events influence independent future outcomes (the gambler's fallacy)
money_quotes: It seems to me like a fairly blatant attempt to build the world's largest, richest consumer database, and then make fabulous profits mining it.
The scariest thing is that Microsoft is advertising Passport as if it were a benefit to consumers, and people seem to be falling for it!
It's a terrifying threat to everyone's personal privacy and it will make today's 'cookies' seem positively tame by comparison.
I'm not a statistician, but I do know that the probability of the Concorde crashing twice is infinitesimal.
Even if you don't know basic probability, do you really think that the Concorde you're flying on knows about the other Concorde that crashed?
key_insight: When a company packages mass data collection as a consumer convenience, people willingly surrender their privacy — a pattern Joel identified in 2000 that would define the next two decades of tech.
